course_description: Basic statistical concepts & techniques such as descriptive statistics, probability concepts, theoretical distributions, inferential statistics (confidence intervals & hypothesis testing) are applied in business & economics.

requirements.prerequisites: Maths B or Maths C or MATH1040
requirements.incompatible: CHEE2010 or 3010 or CIVL2530 or MINE3214 or STAT1201 or 1301 or 2201
requirements.assumed_background: Algebra from high school Maths B.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
As this is entirely an online course, no timetabling for the course is available on mySI-net.

Note: the University will be closed during the mid semester break from Monday 24 December to Tuesday 1 January.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of the course is for students to develop an ability to apply inferential statistics techniques to independently solve practical problems and to then explain the solutions using everyday language.

learning_outcomes.description: recognise, interpret and use fundamental statistical terminology in practice;
apply theoretical concepts to construct analytical statistical techniques;
describe the statistical techniques needed to solve particular problem types;
conduct statistical analysis of data so as to draw statistical conclusions;
communicate statistical findings for practical and professional use.

learning_resources.required_resources:

The required textbook, Black et al. (2016) Australasian Business Statistics , 4th Edition, John Wiley Australia, is available free to all students enrolled in ECON1310 via the Blackboard site. It is this textbook that the lectures and tutorials refer to during the course.

learning_activities.topic: Lectures 1 & 2 (Lecture): Descriptive Statistics 1 & 2 Lecture 1 - Descriptive Statistics 1: statistical concepts and definitions; data types, sources, levels; descriptive & inferential statistics; sampling methods and errors. Lecture 2 - Descriptive Statistics 2: measures of central tendency, variation and shape for ungrouped data; box and whisker plot; linear combination of random variables; coefficient of correlation. Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 1, Ch 3, Ch 7.1 - 7.3)
Mid Semester Break and Public Holidays (Lecture): No lectures this week.

Lectures 7 & 8 (Lecture): Confidence Intervals 1 & 2 Lecture 7 - Confidence Intervals 1: confidence interval estimate for the mean using both Z and t distributions; confidence interval for proportion.

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Feedback in this Course

Feedback is obtained from the CML quizzes, and via consultation with tutors. A viewing session will be held for the final exam - further details will be bulk-emailed to student email addresses at the relevant time.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other Course Guidelines

In the School of Economics there is only one brand and model of calculator allowed into examinations without a university approved label, that is the Casio FX82 series (82 with any letters) calculators . Refer to Section 3.4 for further details.

Next Summary
course_introduction:
ECON1310 is an introductory course in quantitative analysis for business and economics. The course covers a variety of techniques applicable to the presentation, interpretation and use of data. The main emphasis is inferential statistics with estimation and hypothesis testing techniques being an important part of the course. Inferential statistics is continued in the simple linear regression topic. There is an emphasis on the use of Excel for analysis and presentation.

This course is widely regarded as challenging by many students. One reason is that the work is very cumulative. This means that it is exceptionally difficult to catch up if a student gets behind in the work. Success in the subject depends on keeping up-to-date. With this in mind, each topic is progressively assessed via a computer managed quiz.

As well as covering concepts that are an essential part of the analytical tool box for well-trained professionals, ECON1310 provides a foundation that is critical for success in later statistical courses, particularly ECON2300 Introductory Econometrics.

Since ECON1310 is an external course over summer semester, the final exam can be completed off-campus at a number of venues across Australia and overseas. For more information about off-campus examinations, including a list of venues, please read the following webpage: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/off-campus-examinations

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This is the third Summer semester that ECON1310 is being offered entirely as an online course. There will be no face to face contact, meaning there will be no lectures or tutorials to attend on campus.

All lectures will ONLY be available online . This mode of course delivery was first offered successfully to students in Summer semester 2, 2016. Students will be able to access all live lecture and course specific YouTube video recordings, a free textbook , and all other required learning materials fully online. To enable the lectures to be delivered online, the School of Economics has developed a purpose built Blackboard site (used for the first time over the summer of 2016 and every semester since then). The purpose of this Blackboard design has been to allow students to study the course online, without having to attend live lectures. More details about how lectures are provided online can be found under section 4.2.

B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .

C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.

D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.

E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Dr Wasanthi Thenuwara is the Course Coordinator and has oversight of course-related matters and is the contact person for administration matters (including lecture sign-on, non subject matter information, timetables, and examination results).

ECON1310 is a #2 unit course. During an internal mode offering over a 13 week semester, where live lectures and tutorials are delivered, UQ's expectation is that a #2 unit course requires a time allocation of, on average, 10 hours per week per lecture . This 10 hours per week typically involves 4.5 hours each week of face to face contact, consisting of attending a 2 hour lecture, a 1.5 hour tutorial, and a 1 hour PASS class. Students are then expected to complete an additional 5.5 hours of self-directed study that required completing weekly tutorial exercises, reading the textbook, and working on CML assessment quizzes. In this external, intensive mode offering, students will be completing 2 lectures per week , so the expectation is that you would be working at least 20 hours per week . So please give serious consideration as to whether you are willing and able to make this time commitment before enrolling in this summer intensive course. Experience shows that students who do not put in this basic time allocation will struggle. In particular, note that the CML assessment submission dates are very strict, and students will be expected to keep pace with the lectures and other learning materials each week in order to do well on the CML quizzes (see more under CML Quizzes below).

Ensure you attain the marks necessary to achieve the grade to which you aspire. We are aware that some students aspire to a 6 or 7, whilst others aspire to a 4. All such aspirations are fine with us. Our job is to clearly specify the grade cut-offs, clearly state the marking criteria and then mark assessment according to this criteria. It is inevitable that some students will end up close (i.e. within 0.5%) of a grade cut-off. This is not unusual or unfair. You control your own destiny - ensure you are on the right side of a grade cut-off. Students can, and do, fail ECON1310 by 0.5% or less. Missing out on a grade cut-off by 0.5% or less is not grounds for a re-mark or any special consideration. Assessment results will only be revisited if a marking error has occurred (e.g. assessment has not been graded according to the stated marking criteria), or if an administrative error has occurred (e.g. results for individual assessment pieces have not been added up correctly). If you feel that either of these has occurred, contact us immediately. After each assessable item, students will be given the opportunity to view their assessment (final exam-viewing session times and locations will be posted on Blackboard) and so satisfy themselves that a marking or administrative error has not occurred. The formal process of querying a course result (requesting a remark on academic grounds) is set out in UQ Policy and Procedures Library under the Assessment Policy 3.10.02 ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment ).

Make sure you check your student email and the ECON1310 Blackboard site regularly . All important correspondence will be communicated to students throughout the semester by email and/or via Blackboard.

Lectures

During summer semester, 2018, a full set of recorded live lectures and lecture notes will be available on Blackboard. The lecture recordings are live lectures which were filmed in a previous semester and edited into 5 to 10 minutes segments. The recorded lectures and lecture notes relevant to the lecture 1 (Descriptive Statistic 1) will be available by 1 November 2019. All lecture notes and video recordings and tutorial questions for the first six lectures are made available on Blackboard from the beginning of the semester (26 November 2018). This will help students evaluate early in the semester the amount of material that will be covered before the mid semester break. The materials relevant to the second part (lectures 7 to 12) will be made available on Blackboard before the mid semester break (20 December 2018).

Students sign-on to the course through mySI-net.

Tutorials

There will be no live tutorials offered in this course. One set of tutorial questions with brief answers will be posted on the Blackboard for each lecture topic. A second set of tutorial questions will also be posted on Blackboard with fully worked solutions, and these provide the model answers approach that students should aim to follow to demonstrate understanding in the final exam. You should work through the tutorials regularly and consistently each week as you complete each lecture. Completing the tutorial questions will help you to successfully complete the CML quizzes.

Peer Assisted Study Sessions (PASS)

There will be no PASS classes offered in this course.

CML Quizzes

Online computer generated quizzes form part of the student's individual assessment in this course. CML quizzes should also serve as a valuable learning tool for students, with lectures, tutorial, and online test bank questions being the key for helping solve CML quiz questions. The regular completion of these individual assessment quizzes will assist students in consolidating their learning and provide valuable feedback on areas that may require more work. Students can learn from their mistakes by referring to the correct answers and reworking questions marked incorrect. The correct answers are only available online after the quiz is submitted and only after the quiz closing deadline. These correct answers can be found by clicking the link for the relevant quiz in Mygrades on Blackboard. Students are able to take a second optional attempt at another quiz (which will have a different set of questions) in order to improve their first attempt mark. The best score from the two attempts for each quiz will contribute towards a student's aggregate CML result. For a complete description regarding the CML quizzes opening and closing dates, please read the assessment section of this Course Profile. The instructions for completing and submitting the CML quizzes can be found on the ECON1310 Blackboard site.

Consultation

All consultation will only be online. No face to face consultation will be available in this course .

Students should use the Blackboard Consult Forum if they have any course content questions. Students should monitor this Consult Forum to support other students as well as to enhance their own learning. However, students should not directly post answers to other students’ CML questions. The Consult Forum will be monitored by teaching staff to address particular issues that might be common among a number of students. However, please be aware that teaching staff responses to individual student questions on the Consult Forum will be very limited in this intensive course offering . It is expected students taking this course will be self-directed, independent learners who make full use of the online learning resources and interactions with other students via the Consult Forum.

For technical issues relating to the CML quizzes please email the CML coordinator (see section 1.3). For all other course related matters, please contact the course coordinator (see section 1.3).

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Negligible knowledge and comprehension of statistical concepts; unable to solve applied numerical problems; provides wrong explanations to theory-based questions.

Semester mark between 0% and 19.99%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Little evidence of understanding of statistical concepts; uses incorrect methods in solving applied numerical problems; provides poor explanations to theory-based questions.

Semester mark between 20% and 44.99%.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 45% and 49.99%.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a basic understanding of statistical concepts; uses partly correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides fair explanations to theory-based questions.

Semester mark between 50% and 64.99%. However, if a student receives less than 45% on the final exam AND the combined total marks from the CML quizzes and final exam are larger than 50% , the maximum grade that can be awarded is a grade of 3. Please refer to Section 5.4.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a fair understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides satisfactory explanations to theory-based questions.

Semester mark between 65% and 74.99%.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Demonstrates a good understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minor errors or omissions; provides good explanations to theory-based questions.

Semester marks between 75% and 84.99%.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Demonstrates an excellent mastery of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minimal or no errors; provides excellent explanations to theory based questions.

Semester mark of 85% +.

Other Requirements & Comments:

See Section 5.4 below on what is requred to pass this course.
late_submission:
CML Assessment: No late submission is possible. CML access is blocked after the due date and time.

MOSS Book Design Assessment: If you are unable to submit your MOSS Book Design by the due date and time specified in Section 5.1 (e.g., due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced), then you should apply for extension of time via https://my.uq.edu.au/node/218/2#2 with supporting documentation at least 24 hours prior to the submission date . If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. In case of illness, extension cannot exceed the number of days you suffered from the medical condition, as stated on the medical certificate.

Assessment submitted after the due date without prior approval from the course coordinator will be penalised at a rate of 10% of value of the assessment item per day. Assessment submitted after three days from the due date will receive no marks.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

PASSING THE COURSE

In addition to what is outlined in Section 5.2, the following requirements will also apply in determining a student's final grade for this course.

To pass this course, a student must satisfy BOTH the following requirements:

Receive a combined total assessment mark of at least 50 marks out of 100 (50% or more) from their marks obtained from the CML quizzes, MOSS Book and the final exam.

Receive at least 27 marks out of 60 marks on the final exam. This means a student must receive 45% or more on the final exam to pass this course. This places a strong emphasis on the need for a student to do well in the final exam.

For example, a student scoring 38 out of 40 for the CML quizzes plus the MOSS Book and only 25 marks out of 60 marks on the final exam, would have an overall combined assessment score of 63 out of 100 (63%). However, in this case, the student would fail the course, and receive a maximum possible grade of 3 since the student has not reached the minimum requirement of 27 marks out of 60 on the final exam.

As another example, consider a student scoring 21 out of 40 for the CML quizzes plus the MOSS Book and 23 marks out of 60 marks on the final exam. The student would have an overall combined assessment score of 44 out of 100. This student would fail the course. This is because the combined assessment mark is not at least 50 marks (and it is also the case that the final exam mark is less than 27 marks). A grade of 2 would be awarded to this student, using the combined CML quiz mark and the final exam mark of 44, by applying the details shown in Section 5.2.

As a final example, consider a student scoring 12 out of 40 for the CML quizzes plus the MOSS Book and 38 marks out of 60 marks on the final exam. The student would have an overall combined assessment score of 50 out of 100. This student would pass the course as both requirements 1 and 2 have been met. A grade of 4 would be awarded to this student, using the combined CML quiz mark and final exam mark of 50, by applying the details shown in Section 5.2.

CML Quizzes

Step by step Instructions for the CML quizzes will be posted on the ECON1310 Blackboard site.

All six (6) CML quiz marks will be used in calculating the student's aggregate mark. The CML quizzes contribute 30% towards the final grade for this course.

An optional second attempt is allowed for all quizzes. A student's best score from the two (2) attempts for each quiz will contribute towards their aggregate CML result.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to completed a particular CML Quiz, or sit the Final Exam, as a result of illness or some other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced (e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to submit a specially designed late CML Quiz, or sit a deferred Final Exam at a later time.

Information on grounds for applying for deferred examinations can be viewed here:

https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/deferring-exam?page=2995

How to apply for a deferred exam can be found on the web at:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/deferred-application-2995

Please read this information very carefully before submitting your request.

Requests for deferred examinations and supporting documentation must be submitted online via mySI-net > myRequests tab

https://www.sinet.uq.edu.au/psp/ps/?cmd=login

Requests for Deferral of End of Semester Final Examinations apply to examinations held at the end of semester during the University’s examination period.

If you are approved a deferred examination, you must be available to sit the deferred examination at the scheduled time. The deferred examination would be your final opportunity to attempt the examination, as you cannot again defer your deferred examination.

Application rulings :

Application rulings will be notified to you by email to your University student email address. Should you not receive a response to your application for an end of semester deferred examination by the time the final results are released, please contact Examinations Section or your faculty, as appropriate.

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you may be eligible to sit a supplementary exam. You can find information about supplementary assessment at: https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/supplementary-assessment?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for supplementary assessment. Also see Supplementary Assessment Procedures https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.09-supplementary-assessment , and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary assessment must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results .

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment, including the CML Quizzes, and MOSS BOOK Design do not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans ( Saturday 9 March 2019 ).

Students with approved deferred/supplementary examinations are expected to be available on these days, no deferrals or off-campus sittings will be granted.
